topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The aroma of old paper and strong coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I found the manuscript tucked beneath a stack of gardening magazines—a sprawling, intimate narrative detailing my life, my quirks, my infuriating habits. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded as I stared at the title page. Every meticulously crafted sentence was a direct assault on my privacy. I picked up a stray bookmark, the edges rough against my fingertips, and felt a cold, sharp resentment bloom in my chest. I didn't look up as Mark entered, whistling tunelessly, completely oblivious to the evidence lying on the desk. When he finally spoke, his voice light and breezy, I managed a tight, brittle smile that didn't reach my eyes. "Oh, that old thing? Just some old scribbles," I said, my tone carefully neutral, though my fingers clenched the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white.

[story 2]

I traced the lines of the dedication page with a fingertip, a slow, deliberate movement that felt like scraping sandpaper across my nerves. The sheer audacity of it—my entire existence, distilled into prose by the man I shared my life with. A hot flush crept up my neck, quickly followed by a deep, internal settling of icy resolve. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. "You know," I managed, my voice dangerously level, "this is incredibly invasive." I didn't wait for a response. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling a powerful urge to shatter something fragile. The thought of him, sitting there, crafting my flaws into plot points, fueled a deep, burning dissatisfaction that settled low in my stomach, a heavy, unwelcome weight.

[story 3]

He was in the kitchen, humming while preparing breakfast, entirely unaware that the foundation of his morning routine was a thinly veiled biography of my most embarrassing moments. I watched him, my gaze lingering just long enough to register the casualness of his presence, the complete lack of awareness. A slow, deliberate sigh escaped me, a sound laced with profound irritation. I picked up the bound volume, feeling the weight of it—a tangible representation of my subjugation to his imagination. My hands trembled slightly as I flipped through a passage describing a moment of profound vulnerability I thought was private. I slammed the book shut with enough force to make the ceramic mug rattle on the counter. I turned away, walking toward the living room with a stiff, deliberate gait, every step radiating a tightly controlled, simmering displeasure.
